Feasibility of Recruitment, Perceived Genetic Risk and Susceptibility Beliefs, and Saliva Sample Collection in a Community-Based Prostate Cancer Genetic Risk Pilot Study

evidence_entities.text: Prostate cancer is the most common cancer and the second leading cause of cancer-related deaths among men in the United States
Risk factors for developing prostate cancer include older age, race/ethnicity, lifestyle factors, family history of prostate cancer, and prostate cancer genetics
While screening for prostate cancer through prostate-specific antigen (PSA) testing can lower mortality rates by increasing early detection, the benefits of screening continue to be debated

Genome-wide association studies and fine-mapping efforts performed by the prostate cancer consortium (PRACTICAL) have identified 269 single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) associated with prostate cancer susceptibility

Black men are significantly underrepresented in genetic research due to historical mistrust of medical research, lack of awareness about genetics and genetic testing, concerns regarding data usage, and limited access to research opportunities
A total of 154 individuals were reached and 100 consented (62 of whom were Black). In-person recruitment was associated with higher enrollment
Black males reported greater perceived risk compared with white men, but saliva sample return was comparable (89% and 90%, respectively)
Overall, the saliva sample return rate was 85%, with an in-person return rate of 95%, and a mail return rate of 69%
